config.title: Berlin Marathon 2026: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on the date, what closes and when it reopens, how to cross the course, where to watch, and what marathon weekend does to an ordinary Berlin trip.

items.a: The Berlin Marathon 2026 takes place on Sunday 27 September 2026. The main field starts at 09:15, and wheelchair racers and handbikes go first at around 08:50. The start and the finish are both on Straße des 17. Juni, on the stretch between the Brandenburg Gate and the Kleiner Stern. The weekend also includes events on Saturday 26 September, including a 5K along the last five kilometres of the course, a mini marathon and the inline skating marathon.

items.q: Which parts of Berlin are closed for the marathon?
items.a: The course is a 42 kilometre loop through the middle of the city, so closures reach Tiergarten, Mitte, Friedrichshain, Kreuzberg, Neukölln, Schöneberg, Steglitz, Wilmersdorf and Zehlendorf. The heaviest and longest closures are in Tiergarten and Mitte, because that is where the start, the finish and the final kilometres are. The exact 2026 closure map is published by the organiser and by Berlin’s traffic information centre in September.

items.q: What time do the roads reopen after the Berlin Marathon?
items.a: They do not all reopen together, and they do not reopen when the winner finishes. Following the pattern Berlin’s traffic centre published for the 2025 race, Friedrichshain and Kreuzberg come back first from around 14:00, Tempelhof-Schöneberg from about 16:30, Mitte and Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf from roughly 17:30, and Unter den Linden last at about 19:00. Straße des 17. Juni itself stays closed for days afterwards while the finish area is dismantled.

items.q: How do I cross the marathon course?
items.a: Use the U-Bahn or S-Bahn. Both run normally on marathon day and both run underneath the course, so one or two stops takes you from one side to the other without going near a barrier. The Tiergarten tunnel also stays open. There are stewarded surface crossings too: in 2025 the marked Sunday crossings were at Kurfürstendamm and Tauentzienstraße before Rankestraße, at Wittenbergplatz, at Potsdamer Platz by the U-Bahn station traffic light, at Leipziger Straße and Friedrichstraße, and at Französische Straße and Friedrichstraße. Do not try to walk around the course, because it loops.

items.q: Do buses and trams run during the Berlin Marathon?
items.a: They run, but they are diverted, and the diversions change through the day as the police release sections of the course. A route shown in a journey app may not match what the bus actually does. On marathon Sunday the U-Bahn and S-Bahn are the reliable layer, and they are also the only layer that gets you across the course.

items.q: Where is the best place to watch the Berlin Marathon?
items.a: Go west and go late. The Memorial Church sits at about km 35 and Potsdamer Platz at km 38.5, and by the time the main field reaches them the crowd has thinned enough to get to the front of the barrier. The Reichstag at around km 6.5 and Friedrichstadtpalast at km 8 fill up early while the field is still dense, and the Brandenburg Gate finish at km 42 is the most crowded point of the whole day.

items.q: Does the Berlin Marathon affect Saturday as well?
items.a: Yes, and this is the part most visitors miss. The inline skating marathon uses the full course on Saturday afternoon, and in 2025 it closed Straße des 17. Juni, Großer Stern, Ernst-Reuter-Platz, Otto-Suhr-Allee, Hofjägerallee, Reichpietschufer, Potsdamer Straße, Leipziger Straße, Jerusalemer Straße, Markgrafenstraße, Französische Straße, Glinkastraße and Unter den Linden. Unter den Linden between Pariser Platz and Friedrichstraße was closed from 07:00 to 18:00. If that is your one Saturday in Berlin, do the historic centre in the morning.

items.q: Is the Tiergarten closed before the marathon?
items.a: The park itself is open, but the start and finish build-up takes over the central axis for roughly two weeks. In 2025, Straße des 17. Juni between Yitzhak-Rabin-Straße and the Brandenburg Gate was closed from 13 September until 24 September, with Großer Stern and Yitzhak-Rabin-Straße joining from 17 September. If you visit Berlin in the second half of September, expect fencing, grandstands and trucks around the Victory Column and the central axis.

items.q: Can I still visit museums and sights on marathon Sunday?
items.a: Yes. Museums are indoors and unaffected, and Museum Island, Hackescher Markt and the area east of Alexanderplatz stay workable through the day. The practical rule is to pick one side of the course and stay on it until the afternoon, then use the fact that Friedrichshain and Kreuzberg reopen first if you want to move east later.

items.q: How many people run the Berlin Marathon?
items.a: It is one of the World Marathon Majors and one of the largest marathons in the world. More than 50,000 participants took part in 2025, drawn from around 120 countries, with roughly a million spectators along the course. The course is flat and fast, which is why records happen here: Eliud Kipchoge set the course record of 2:01:09 in 2022, and Sabastian Sawe won in 2:02:16 in 2025.
